mechanism_narrative: A month-over-month increase in nonfarm payroll employment (PAYEMS) signals expanding labor income and consumption demand, which translates into higher expected corporate revenues and positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: S&P 500 20-day forward returns are not positively correlated with month-over-month PAYEMS changes (Spearman rank correlation < 0.03) over a 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate (UNRATE) rises by more than 0.3 percentage points over a trailing three-month window, it triggers a recession warning that reduces risk appetite and drives negative S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days.
failure_condition: In instances where the trigger activates, S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive more than 50% of the time, or the mean forward return is not significantly negative (t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: The interaction of elevated consumer price inflation (CPIAUCSL above its 12-month trailing average) and a low federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS below 0.5%) creates negative real interest rates that push capital out of fixed income and into equities, generating positive S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days.
failure_condition: When both conditions are met, S&P 500 5-day forward returns are negative in more than 45% of observations, or the interaction term adds no incremental explanatory power over main effects (F-test p > 0.05) in a 3-year rolling regression.

mechanism_narrative: Month-over-month growth in real retail sales (RSAFS) predicts positive S&P 500 returns, but this relationship is only reliable during post-recession recovery phases when pent-up demand drives sustained consumption acceleration; outside this regime the signal is noise.
failure_condition: During post-recession recovery periods identified by NBER chronology, the rank correlation between RSAFS changes and 20-day SPX returns is below 0.05, or the directional accuracy is not greater than 55%.

mechanism_narrative: A declining industrial production index (INDPRO) signals negative supply shocks and weakening manufacturing profitability that erode earnings expectations, causing negative S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days.
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day forward returns following month-over-month INDPRO declines are not significantly negative (t-test p > 0.05) over a 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) falling below 900 thousand units signals a freeze in residential investment and construction employment that drags down cyclical earnings, producing negative S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days.
failure_condition: When HOUST is below 900, S&P 500 5-day forward returns are positive in more than 50% of instances, or the mean forward return is not negative (t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneous month-over-month increases in retail sales (RSAFS) and housing starts (HOUST) confirm a broad-based demand expansion that reduces recession probability and drives positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days beyond what either series predicts alone.
candidate_trigger: Month-over-month increase in RSAFS and month-over-month increase in HOUST
failure_condition: The joint signal of RSAFS and HOUST increases does not outperform the best single-proxy signal by at least 3 percentage points in directional accuracy over a 3-year rolling window, or the interaction term is insignificant (p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: Quarter-over-quarter growth in real GDP (GDPC1) above 2.5% annualized signals that aggregate demand is running sufficiently hot to support top-line revenue growth across the economy, leading to positive S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days.
failure_condition: When GDPC1 growth exceeds 2.5%, S&P 500 60-day forward returns are negative in more than 45% of triggered instances, or the mean forward return is not significantly positive (t-test p > 0.05).
